segments.s079-g81: فرو ریخت is a compound of the adverb فرو (down) + the past verb ریخت (poured/fell), literally 'poured down' — ریخت is 3rd person singular past. از مژه سیندخت is a prepositional phrase 'from Sindokht's eyelash' placed before the noun خون, and خون (blood) is the thing pouring (here presented after the verb for poetic emphasis rather than the more neutral subject–verb order). سیندخت is a proper name.
segments.s079-g82: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/because' or 'that'), here giving the reason or context. کودک ز پهلو is 'the child from (her) side' with ز as the poetic form of 'from'. کی آید برون is an interrogative subordinate clause: کی = 'when', آید is the present/indicative 3rd person of آمدن used here to mean 'will come', and برون = 'out/forth' follows the verb.
segments.s079-g25: همان (=“that very/the same”) highlights the particular feather. پر سیمرغش is a noun phrase 'the Simorgh's feather' with the possessive enclitic -ش referring to the bird; آمد is the past tense 'came'. The adverbial phrase به یاد follows the verb in this older/poetic word order: به یاد آمد = 'came to mind' (i.e. it was remembered/occurred to them).
segments.s079-g26: بخندید is the past tense 'laughed' (3rd person). The conjunction و links the two coordinated actions. سیندخت را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker after the proper name; مژده داد is the verb phrase 'gave tidings/gave good news' with داد as the past 'gave' (not 'justice').
segments.s079-g27: یکی is an indefinite 'one/somebody.' مجمر آورد is a common past construction 'brought a brazier' (object before the verb in Persian classical style is also possible but here noun+verb). The second clause آتش فروخت uses فروخت in the older/poetic sense 'kindled/caused to blaze' rather than the more common 'sold.'
segments.s079-g28: وزآن = و + از آن 'and from that'; پر سیمرغ names the object 'the Simorgh's feather.' لختی is an adverbial 'for a short while,' and بسوخت is the past intransitive 'it burned/was burned.' The clause describes a brief burning of that feather.
segments.s079-g29: هم = 'also' sets an additive tone. اندر زمان = 'in/at the time' (classical preposition اندر). تیره گون is a split adjective (تیره + گون) meaning 'dark-hued' or 'turned dark' and شد is the past 'became.' هوا here means 'the air/atmosphere or mood' — so 'the air/mood became dark.'
segments.s079-g30: پدید آمد is a compound verbal expression 'appeared / came into view.' آن مرغ فرمان روا is a nominal phrase: آن مرغ = 'that bird' and فرمان روا is the poetic split form of فرمانروا meaning 'ruler' or 'sovereign' (literally 'command-holder'); together: 'that sovereign bird appeared.'
segments.s079-g31: چو = 'like/as.' ابری here is 'a cloud' (noun). که introduces a relative clause 'whose'; بارانش = 'its rain' (possessive -ش). مرجان is a metaphorical object 'coral' (used poetically as jewels/pearls) and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause means 'like a cloud whose rain was coral.'
segments.s079-g32: چه here is an exclamatory 'what/how' emphasizing the following noun. مرجان repeats 'coral' and که links to the explanatory clause. آرایش جان is a genitive phrase 'the adornment/ornament of the soul' and بود is the past copula 'was' — 'what coral! for it was the ornament of the soul.'
segments.s079-g17: چنان بد که یک روز ازو رفت هوش is a result clause introduced by که: چنان بد ('so/so bad') sets up the severity, یک روز ('one day') gives the time, ازو رفت هوش is idiomatic Persian word order where the possessed noun هوش ('awareness/sense') follows رفت ('went/left') and the agent/possessor ازو ('from him') is placed before the verb — together meaning 'his sense left him' (i.e. he lost consciousness).
segments.s079-g18: از ایوان دستان برآمد خروش is a simple nominal phrase + verb: از ایوان ('from the porch') is a prepositional source, دستان modifies ایوان (poetic/localized name/description), and برآمد خروش is a verbal clause in which برآمد ('arose/issued forth') takes خروش ('a cry/roar') as its subject/object — 'a cry rose from the porch.'
segments.s079-g19: خروشید سیندخت و بشخود روی places the verb خروشید ('cried out/shouted') before the proper name (verb–subject order common in epic poetry). و بشخود ('and to herself') is a reflexive adverbial phrase preparing the following action, and روی ('face') is the noun that will combine with the verb in the next segment to mean 'turn her face.'
segments.s079-g20: بکند آن سیه گیسوی مشک بوی contains the verb بکند (classical/poetic form of کردن with the b- prefix) which here means 'turn'/'do' combined with the previous noun روی to yield 'turn (her) face.' آن سیه گیسوی مشک بوی is the direct object/phrase describing what is presented to the face: آن ('that') + سیه گیسوی ('black lock of hair') + مشک بوی (postpositive compound meaning 'musk‑scented').
segments.s079-g21: یکایک بدستان رسید آگهی uses یکایک ('one by one/each') to quantify بدستان (here an epithet meaning 'the ill‑fated' or 'unlucky ones'); رسید ('reached/came') is the verb and آگهی ('news/information') is the item that arrived. Word order places the affected people before the verb, so the clause means 'news reached each of the ill‑fated.'
segments.s079-g22: که پژمرده شد برگ سرو سهی is a reported clause introduced by که ('that'). پژمرده شد is a two‑word verbal phrase meaning 'became withered' or 'was withered' (شد as auxiliary/result), and برگ سرو سهی is a poetic noun phrase 'the leaf of the dark/black cypress' with سهی postpositively qualifying سرو ('dark/black').
segments.s079-g23: به بالین رودابه شد زال زر places the locative phrase به بالین رودابه ('to Rudabeh's bedside') at the front, then the verb شد ('came/occurred' used narratively) and the subject زال زر ('Zal, (in) gold' — i.e. Zal clad in gold or accompanied by gold). The verb–subject inversion is typical in poetry and the noun زر functions as an epithet/qualification of Zal.
segments.s079-g24: پر از آب رخسار و خسته جگر is a pair of linked descriptive phrases about Zal: پر از آب رخسار ('cheek(s) full of water') uses پر از ('full of') + آب ('water/tears') with رخسار ('countenance/cheek') to mean 'tears running down his face', and خسته جگر ('weary‑liver') is a poetic collocation where جگر ('liver') stands for the seat of feeling (heart) and خسته ('weary') precedes it — 'with a weary heart.'
segments.s079-g41: از آواز او is a causal/trigger phrase: از = 'from' (here 'at/through'), آواز او = 'his voice/call'. چرم جنگی is a noun phrase 'war(-)hide' (چرم modified by the adjective/attributive جنگی). پلنگ is the predicate noun that the hide will become; the copular verb is postponed to the next segment (شود).
segments.s079-g42: شود is the classical 3rd-person singular present/preterite auxiliary meaning 'becomes'. چاک چاک is a reduplicated adjective/adverb meaning 'torn to shreds' (emphatic). و = 'and'. بخاید is the 3rd-person singular of خاییدن here meaning 'gape' or 'open wide' (applied to claws). دو چنگ = 'two claws' (numeral + noun); the verb precedes its object, a normal Classical Persian word order.
segments.s079-g43: هران گرد is a poetic phrase meaning 'all around' / 'on every side' (هران = 'every', گرد = 'around'). کاواز is an obscure word here treated as a name or fixed term; کوپال اوی is a possessive sequence: کوپال (a personal name) + اوی (classical enclitic = 'his/its'), so 'Koopal's Kaavaz' (the noun کاواز is modified by that possession). The full chunk describes something encircling or surrounding.
segments.s079-g44: ببیند is a 3rd-person singular present 'he/one sees' (used narratively, often impersonal 'one sees' or 'it is seen'). بر here functions as the locative preposition 'on/upon' introducing the following list of loci. بازوی و یال اوی are nouns joined by و, with اوی = 'his' attached to the final noun and understood to govern the whole series: 'on his breast (بر) and on his arm and on his mane.'
segments.s079-g45: ز آواز او = 'from/because of his voice/call' (ز = poetic 'from'). اندر آید is a compound of اندر = 'into/within' + آید = 'comes', so 'enters' or 'comes into'. ز پای here identifies the target locus 'into (the) feet' (literally 'from voice there comes into the feet'), i.e., a trembling/motion reaches the feet.
segments.s079-g46: دل مرد جنگی is a genitive/construct: دل = 'heart' modified by مرد جنگی = 'the war-man / warrior' (مرد جنگی = valiant man). برآید is the verbal form of برآمدن 'to rise/jump up' in 3rd-person singular ('rises, starts up'). ز جای = 'from its place' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place') describing where the heart 'rises' from.
segments.s079-g47: به جای خرد = 'in place of wisdom' / 'instead of wisdom' (به جای as a compound meaning 'in place of', خرد = 'wisdom'). سام is the proper-name subject. سنگی is a predicative adjective 'stony / stone-hearted' describing Sam. بود is the copula 'was' (past).
segments.s079-g48: به خشم = 'in anger' (به + noun used adverbially). اندرون = 'within/inside' specifying interiority. شیر جنگی = 'a warlike lion' (شیر = 'lion', جنگی = 'warlike / fighter'); بود is again the copula 'was'. The phrase describes Sam's inward state as an angry, warlike lion.
segments.s079-g57: بکافد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive/implied impersonal form meaning "it suffices" or "will suffice" (بـ- prefix + کافد). تهیگاه is the head noun "hollow/void/place" and سرو سهی is a genitive/attributive phrase "the dark cypress" (سرو = cypress, سهی = dark/dusky); classical Persian often omits the ezāfe, so the sequence reads "the hollow-place of the dark cypress."
segments.s079-g58: نباشد is a negative 3rd-person present/subjunctive/existential form meaning "there is not / there will not be" (here: "there will be no"). مر is the dative pronoun "to me," placed before the third‑person pronoun او which bears را as a topical/definite marker; ز introduces the source/relationship ("of/from"), درد is "suffering/affliction," and آگهی means "news/information." Together: "there will be no information to me about his suffering."
segments.s079-g59: بیرون کشد is a 3rd-person present form of کشیدن placed at the end (classical word order: verb final) meaning "draws out / brings forth." بچه شیر is a noun + modifier meaning "a lion's cub" (بچه = young/offspring, شیر = lion). وزو here functions as a prepositional/archaic particle meaning roughly "from it / from there" (an old-fashioned variant of از + pronoun).
segments.s079-g60: همه modifies the following noun phrase (همه پهلوی ماه = "the whole/flank of the moon"); در خون is a prepositional phrase "in/with blood," and کشد (verb-final) means "he drenches/marks/extends" (here: "stains/drenches in blood"). The clause thus describes action affecting the entire flank of the moon.
segments.s079-g61: وز آن پس is a temporal/sequence marker "and/then after that" (وز here is an archaic/poetic variant related to از). بدوز is an imperative (2nd-person singular) meaning "sew (up)" or "mend." آن کجا کرد چاک is a relative clause: آن کجا = "that place/where," کرد is past tense "he made/did," and چاک is "a gash/tear." So: "sew up that place where he made the gash."
segments.s079-g62: ز دل دور کن is an imperative instruction: ز introduces the source "from (the) heart," دل = "heart," دور = "away/put far," and کن is the imperative "do / make" (here: "put away/remove"). The following nouns ترس و تیمار و باک are a coordinate list "fear, solicitude/care, and dread/concern" — the objects to be removed from the heart.
segments.s079-g63: گیاهی که گویمت is a relative clause: گیاهی = "a herb/plant," که introduces the relative clause, and گویمت is 1st-person present/future "I tell you / I will tell you." با شیر و مشک is the instrumental phrase "with milk and musk," listing how the herb is to be treated.
segments.s079-g64: بکوب و بکن are two imperatives: بکوب "pound/crush" and بکن "do/make/prepare." هر سه literally "all three" (هر here functioning as "each/all" to indicate the three ingredients), در سایه = "in the shade," and خشک = "dry." Together: "Pound and prepare all three; dry them in the shade."
segments.s079-g49: Two prepositional phrases joined by و. به بالای سرو = به (preposition) + بالای 'atop/above' + سرو 'the cypress' → 'onto the top of the cypress'. The second phrase به نیروی پیل uses به in the instrumental sense 'by/through' (with نیروی 'strength/force' governed by the genitive relation to پیل 'elephant') → 'by the elephant's strength'.
segments.s079-g50: Main verbal sequence: به آورد خشت افگند. Here به (prepositional 'to/onto') links the action to the place; آورد is past 'brought/placed' and خشت is the object 'brick'. افگند is a past tense verb 'threw/cast' that follows, so the clause reads 'he brought a brick and cast it'. بر دو میل = بر 'on/upon' + دو 'two' + میل 'pillars/masts'.
segments.s079-g51: نیاید is an older/poetic negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not come' or 'will not come' (3rd person). به گیتی = 'into the world' (به + گیتی). ز راه زهش = ز (poetic از) 'from/through' + راه 'way' + زهش 'of it/that' (demonstrative/relativizing clitic), so 'not come into the world by that way.'
segments.s079-g52: An independent clause with an imperative/hortative flavor. به فرمان = 'by/according to the command (of)'; دادار is a title 'the Creator' (genitive relation: فرمان دادار = the Creator's command). نیکی دهش is a concise poetic phrase: نیکی 'goodness' as object and دهش used here as a hortative/nominalized verb 'give/grant' (classical/poetic use) → 'grant goodness' or 'give goodness.'
segments.s079-g53: بیاور is the 2nd-person imperative 'bring!'. یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one/a certain'. خنجر آبگون = noun + adjective (classical order often places the adjective after the noun): خنجر 'dagger' + آبگون 'bluish' (literally 'water-colored'). So: 'Bring a bluish dagger.'
segments.s079-g54: Another coordinated request: یکی مرد = 'one man' / 'a certain man'. The following words بینادل and پرفسون are rare or corrupt epithets in the text; they function as descriptive names or adjectives after 'مرد'. In translation they are left as uncertain epithets attached to that man.
segments.s079-g55: نخستین is an adverb/adjective 'first' or 'first of all'. به می‌ماه را uses به + می‌ماه 'mid-month (the full-moon or fifteenth)' with را marking it as the direct object. مست کن is a causative imperative: مست (adjective 'drunk/intoxicated') + کن (imperative 'make/do') → 'make (it) drunk' i.e. 'intoxicate the mid-month (with wine)'.
segments.s079-g56: ز دل = 'from the heart' (ز = from/of, دل = heart). بیم و اندیشه را = 'fear and thought' marked by را as the object. پست کن again is adjective + کن 'make low/abase' in the imperative → 'abase (or cast down) fear and thought from the heart.' The ordering (source phrase ز دل before object with را) is a poetic inversion common in classical Persian.
segments.s079-g1: بسی برنیامد برین روزگار is a compact, somewhat poetic clause: بسی (much/very/of many) + برنیامد (negative past/perfect sense 'did not appear/was not seen') + برین روزگار (the phrase بر این روزگار = 'in these days/at this time'). Together it expresses that something did not often occur in these times. Note the archaic compression برین = بر + این ('on/at this').
segments.s079-g2: که introduces a explanatory relative clause 'that/which': 'that a noble cypress came into bearing.' آزاده is an adjectival epithet 'noble-hearted / high-minded', سرو is the cypress (a common poetic emblem), اندر آمد = 'came in / came into' and به بار completes the verbal idiom به بار آمدن 'to bear fruit / to come to fruit'. The whole clause states the surprising event that such a cypress began to bear.
segments.s079-g3: بهار دل‌افروز is a nominative noun phrase 'the heart-delighting (cheering) spring'. پژمرده شد is a simple past/passive-like resultative 'became withered / withered'. شد here marks the change of state ('became').
segments.s079-g4: دلش را is 'his heart' with ش = possessive and را marking the definite object/topic; غم و رنج 'sorrow and suffering' is the noun phrase that follows. بسپرده شد is a passive causative/result 'was entrusted / was delivered over', so the clause means 'his heart was handed over to sorrow and suffering.' Note the compound passive sense where بسپرده (past participle) + شد expresses being made subject to that state.
segments.s079-g5: شکم گشت فربه is 'the belly turned/fell to being fat' (گشت = 'became/turned'); و connects the second coordinated clause تن شد گران 'the body became heavy' where شد again marks the change of state. This is a straightforward parallel sequence of resultatives.
segments.s079-g6: شد introduces another result clause 'became': آن ارغوانی رخش زعفران = 'that purple of his rukh/face became saffron.' رخش is used as 'his countenance/cheek/beauty' and زعفران indicates the saffron (yellowish) color. The word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb first, then demonstrative + adjectives + noun + complement).
segments.s079-g7: بدو = 'to him' (dative), گفت is the past 'said' (reporting verb), مادر is the subject 'the mother'. The following که‌ای is a compact marker of reported speech + vocative: که (introducing the quoted clause) + ای (vocative) and جان مام is the vocative 'O my life / my dear' with مام a dialectal vocative for 'mother/parent' or a familiar address. The phrase is mother → speech → vocative.
segments.s079-g8: چه بودت introduces an interrogative 'what was with you / what ailed you' (چه = 'what/how' as exclamation/interrogative; بودت = 'was to you / happened to you' using the old pronominal ending -ت). که here links to the result clause 'that you became...' and گشتی (in context = 'you became') is the past of گشتن used as 'become' in poetry; چنین زرد فام = 'so pale/having such a hue', with چنین 'so' and فام 'hue/complexion'. The whole line asks why the addressee has grown so pale.
segments.s079-g33: کرد is the past tense verb 'did/made' taking the following noun phrase as its object: 'آفرین دراز' = 'a long afrin/praise'. The initial برو is an isolated particle here (poetic/archaic) whose precise force is uncertain in this context (see note) and does not change the main past construction 'کرد + object'; 'زال' is the agent placed between the verb and its object (poetic word order).
segments.s079-g34: ستودش is the past tense 'he praised him' (ستود + enclitic ـش = 'him'); فراوان is an adverbial modifier 'abundantly/much'. و links to a second coordinated verb phrase. بردش is the past of بردن 'to take/bring' with the object enclitic ـش 'him' (poetic order places object after the verb here) and نماز is the destination/object 'to (the) prayer' — together 'and he took him to prayer / led him in prayer.'
segments.s079-g35: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech verb. گفت is the past 'said' (classical past of گفتن) and با زال marks the addressee 'to/with Zal' (با + name used for 'to' someone in speech). کین غم is a nominal phrase 'this hatred/sorrow' and چراست is the interrogative copula 'why is (it)?' — together forming the question 'why is this sorrow?'.
segments.s079-g36: به marks location ('in' or 'within') and combines with چشم هژبر 'the eye of Hejbar' (چشم + genitive/name). اندرون 'inside/within' reinforces the locative. نم here means 'tear/moisture' (a tear in the eye) and چراست again asks 'why is it?'. Word order is poetic: locative phrase precedes the questioned predicate.
segments.s079-g37: کزین is a classical prepositional compound 'from this/that'. سرو سیمین is a poetic epithet ('silver/silvery cypress') used metaphorically for a tall/beautiful person; بر + ماه‌روی links the image to 'moon-faced' ('with a moonlike face'). The whole phrase describes origin/appearance: 'from this silvery cypress with moonlike face'.
segments.s079-g38: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain'. نره شیر is a compound 'male lion' (نره qualifying شیر), and آید is the present/past classical verb 'comes/appears'. The conjunction و links the appositive name that follows: نامجوی is a proper name (Namjuy) identifying that lion — 'a male lion named Namjuy appears.'
segments.s079-g39: که functions as a relative/complementizer 'who/that'. خاک پی او is a genitival phrase 'the dust at his foot / the dust following him' (خاک + پی او 'after him/at his feet'), and ببوسد is a third‑person singular form (classical subjunctive/present) 'may/might kiss' or 'would kiss'; finally هژبر is the subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion): 'that Hejbar would kiss the dust at his feet.'
segments.s079-g40: This clause is heavily inverted for poetic effect. گذشتن is the infinitive 'to pass', and ابر 'cloud' is the logical subject; به سر برش = 'over his head' (به سر = 'to/over the head', برش = بر + ـش 'on/over him'). نیارد is an archaic/poetic negative/auxiliary here (uncertain exact form) that gives the sense 'does not/dare not / will not let' — so the combined meaning is 'a cloud will not be able to pass over his head.' Note: several items are in poetic order and some forms are archaic, so the clause reads more smoothly when reordered into modern prose.
segments.s079-g9: 'چنین داد پاسخ که' is a poetic inversion meaning 'he gave such an answer that…'. چنين (thus) comes before the verbal phrase داد پاسخ (gave an answer / replied); که introduces the reported clause. 'من روز و شب' is the subject plus a time adverbial ('I, day and night').
segments.s079-g10: همی indicates a continuous/habitual action (classical present/future). برگشایم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive of برگشودن ('to open' / 'undo') used here as 'I will open / utter' (poetic usage). به فریاد لب is a prepositional phrase ('to/with a cry of the lip') — idiomatically 'to cry out' — where به marks the means ('with/in') and لب ('lip') completes the image.
segments.s079-g11: همانا is an emphatic 'verily' or 'truly'. زمان here = 'the time' or 'the moment' and is the logical subject; آمدستم is an archaic first‑person perfective 'I came/arrived'. فراز ('to the summit / up high') follows, giving the sense 'I have come up to the summit' (poetic word order: adverbial/final position).
segments.s079-g12: وزین بار 'a heavy load' (وزین = weighty; بار = load). بردن is the infinitival/nominal form 'to carry/bear'; نیابم is the negative present/subjunctive/ability form meaning 'I do not find / I cannot obtain' (archaic spelling/formation), and جواز is the object 'permission/means/justification'. The literal order is 'for carrying this heavy load I find no permission', i.e. 'I cannot find means to bear this heavy burden.'
segments.s079-g13: تو گویی here functions as 'you say' (poetic 2nd‑person present). به سنگستم is a resultative state 'I have become/turned to stone' (سنگستم = 'I became stone' with first‑person ending), and آگنده پوست is an adjectival phrase 'skin filled' (آگنده + پوست) meaning 'my skin has become filled/hardened' — the adjective precedes the noun in this poetic phrasing.
segments.s079-g14: و گر = 'and if / but if'. آهنست is a nominal predicate 'is of iron' (آهن + -ست). آنکه = 'he who / that one' and نیز = 'also'. اندروست is an archaic/fused form (likely from 'ان در اوست' = 'that is in him'); its exact lexical identity is uncertain, but functionally it links back to the prior clause: 'that same quality is in him too.'
segments.s079-g15: چنین 'thus/so' begins the clause; تا گه زادن = 'until the time/occasion of giving birth' (تا = until, گه = moment/occasion, زادن = childbirth). آمد is 'came/arrived' and فراز ('up/high') is a poetic locution placed after the verb — overall: 'thus, until the moment of birth came up.'
segments.s079-g16: به خواب و به آرام are two prepositional phrases 'for/to sleep and to rest/quiet', joined by و. بودش is the older third‑person imperfective/perfective copula + enclitic ('it was to her' = 'she had'), and نیاز is the noun 'need'. The whole phrase literally: 'to sleep and to rest was her need' → 'she needed sleep and rest.'
segments.s079-g65: بساو (a poetic verb) here means ‘to be abundant / to sprout abundantly’ and is used impersonally of the plant. و is the simple coordinating 'and'. برآلای is a substantive phrase 'on the brow/crest' (the top or crown of the plant); بر is the preposition 'on/upon' repeated for emphasis or rhythmic balance, and خستگیش is خستگی + ـِش 'its weariness/fatigue' (possessive suffix) describing the place where the crest appears.
segments.s079-g66: ببینی is a 2nd-person present/future-form (optative/indicative in epic Persian) 'you will see/you see'. همان روز is a time phrase 'that same day' (همان = 'the same/that', روز = 'day'). پیوستگیش is پیوستگی + ـِش 'its joining/attachment' (here 'the graft-union' or 'attachment'), the thing you are said to see.
segments.s079-g67: بدو is to+it (به + او → 'to it' / 'for it'), here marking that something belongs to the plant. مال = 'property/possession'; ازان پس = 'from that time on' (از آن + پس = 'after that'); یکی = 'one'; پر = 'feather'; من = 'my' (possessive reference: 'one of my feathers'). The word order is dative ('to it') + possession + time phrase + item possessed.
segments.s079-g68: خجسته بود is a past-tense clause 'was auspicious/blessed' (خجسته = auspicious; بود = 'was'). سایه فر من is a genitive chain: سایه = 'shade/protection', فر = 'royal glory' (the divine/royal radiance), من = 'my' (possessive); together 'the protective shade of my (royal) glory'. The whole clause states that this protective shade was felicitous/blessed.
segments.s079-g69: ترا + را here functions together as the dative/object 'to you' (ت = 2nd-person clitic, را = object/topic marker attached in older texts). زین سخن = 'from/at this speech/news' (زین = 'of/from this' in classical usage; سخن = 'word/news'). شاد باید بدن is an idiomatic obligation: باید = 'ought/should', while بدن in older epic usage functions with باید to express the state 'to be' (i.e. 'you ought to be glad'). In modern Persian one would expect 'شاد باشی'; here classical syntax uses باید + (infinitive/old form) to express the obligation to be in that state.
segments.s079-g70: به پیش = 'into the presence of / before'; جهاندار = 'the sovereign/king' (literally 'holder of the world' used for the ruler). باید شدن is an obligation with شدن ('to go/appear/become' in this context = 'to present oneself, to go and stand before'); together they instruct that the addressee must go into the sovereign's presence. Note the classical use of شدن for motion/appearance.
segments.s079-g71: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/for'. او = 'he'. دادت is the past tense 'gave' with the enclitic ـت indicating the indirect object 'to you' (داد + ـت = 'gave (to you)'). این خسروانی درخت = 'this royal tree' (این = 'this', خسروانی = 'royal', درخت = 'tree'). The clause says that he is the giver of this royal tree.
segments.s079-g72: که again introduces the relative 'whose/which'. هر روز نو = 'every day anew' (هر روز = 'every day', نو = 'new/fresh'). بشکفاندش is a causative/active verb with object suffix ـش 'it' — بشکفاند = 'makes (it) bloom / causes it to open'. بخت = 'fate/fortune' is the agent here ('fortune makes it bloom'), placed at the end for emphasis as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s079-g73: بدین کار (بدین =به + این in classical spelling) is a prepositional phrase 'by/at this matter'; دل is the direct object/experiencer 'heart'; هیچ غمگین مدار is an imperative construction: هیچ 'at all' + غمگین 'sad' + مدار, an archaic negative/jussive imperative meaning 'do not be / do not let (it be)'. The whole segment therefore orders someone not to let their heart be sad about the matter.
segments.s079-g74: که introduces a subordinate (causal/explanatory) clause 'for/because'; شاخ 'bough, branch' is the subject; برومندت is the adjective برومند 'flourishing/prosperous' with the 2nd‑person suffix -ت 'your'; آمد is past 'came/arrived'; به بار is an idiomatic phrase 'to bear (fruit)' (بار here = produce/weight). Word order is typical classical Persian: clause marker که + subject + adjective+possessive + verb + complement.
segments.s079-g75: بگفت is the simple past 'he said' or 'he spoke'; و = 'and'; یکی 'one/a certain' modifies پر 'feather'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (modern از); بازو 'arm' is the source phrase. بکند here is a classical past form of کندن 'to pull/pluck' (with the verbal prefix ب‑), so the clause means 'and (he) plucked one feather from his arm.'
segments.s079-g76: فگند is the past 'he threw/cast' (ف‑ variant of the past prefix plus کندن/فکندن); و 'and'; به پرواز 'into flight' (به + verbal noun پرواز); بر شد بلند is a periphrastic expression: بر + شد (شد here as past auxiliary 'became/took to') + بلند 'high', so 'he rose up on high / took to the air.' The sequence describes two quick successive actions.
segments.s079-g77: بشد here is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'he rose / it happened' (in context 'Zāl rose'); زال is the subject (proper name); و 'and'; آن پر او برگرفت is object clause: آن 'that' + پر 'feather' + او 'his' (or 'it' depending on antecedent) + برگرفت 'took up/ seized' (past). The verb برگرفت is a simple past meaning 'took up/took away.'
segments.s079-g78: برفت is a past tense meaning 'he went away/left' (classical بر + رفت); و 'and'; بکرد is the past 'he did' (ب‑ + کردن) introducing a clause 'he did (performed)'; آنچه 'that which / whatever' is the relative pronoun; گفت here is the past 'was said' or 'said' (the token list lacks the verbal sense but here functions as 'what was said'); ای شگفت is a vocative/exclamatory tag 'O wonder!' The clause as a whole: 'he departed and did what was said — O marvel.'
segments.s079-g79: بدان کار is the prepositional phrase 'at that deed/at that sight' (بدان = به + آن); نظاره شد is a passive/experiential construction 'became a spectacle / was watched' (شد as passive/result auxiliary); یک جهان 'a whole world' is the affected group (the world as witness). The verb follows the prepositional phrase, typical of classical word order.
segments.s079-g80: همه 'all/every' modifies دیده 'eyes'; پر here is the adjectival use 'full (of)' and takes a following genitive: خون 'blood' (i.e. 'eyes full of blood'); و links the second coordinated descriptor خسته روان 'weary of spirit' (خسته 'tired' + روان 'soul/spirit'). The phrase describes the crowd's condition: every eye full of blood and every spirit exhausted.

sentences.s079-s41: Blood poured down from Sindokht’s eyelash, for when will the child come forth from her side?
sentences.s079-s13: That very Simorgh feather came to mind; (it) laughed and gave Sindukht the good news.
sentences.s079-s14: Someone brought a brazier and kindled a fire; and from that Simorgh feather it burned for a short while.
sentences.s079-s15: Also, in that time the air grew dark; that sovereign bird appeared.
sentences.s079-s16: Like a cloud whose rain was coral; what coral — for it was the adornment of the soul.
sentences.s079-s9: It was so terrible that one day his senses left him; from the porch a cry rose up.
sentences.s079-s10: Sindukht cried out, and to herself she turned her face toward that musk‑scented black tress.
sentences.s079-s11: One by one the ill‑fated received the news that the leaf of the dark cypress had withered.
sentences.s079-s12: To Rudabeh's bedside came Zal, clad in gold, his cheek full of water and his heart weary.
sentences.s079-s21: At his cry the war-hide becomes a leopard, ripped to shreds, and its two claws gape.
sentences.s079-s22: All around was the ring (or call) of Koopal’s Kaavaz; one saw it on his chest and on his arm and on his mane.
sentences.s079-s23: From his cry a sensation goes into the feet; the heart of the warrior rises from its place.
sentences.s079-s24: In place of wisdom Sam was stony; within, a warlike lion was in anger.
sentences.s079-s29: The hollow of the dark cypress will suffice; I shall have no news of his suffering.
sentences.s079-s30: From it a lion's cub is drawn forth; he drenches the whole flank of the moon in blood.
sentences.s079-s31: Then sew up the place where he made the gash; remove from your heart fear, worry, and dread.
sentences.s079-s32: A herb that I will tell you — pound it with milk and musk; combine all three and dry in the shade.
sentences.s079-s25: Onto the top of the cypress, and by the strength of the elephant, he brought a brick and cast it upon two pillars.
sentences.s079-s26: He does not come into the world by that way; give goodness at the command of the Creator.
sentences.s079-s27: Bring one bluish dagger; one man — (the damaged/uncertain epithets follow).
sentences.s079-s28: First, intoxicate the mid-month with wine; from the heart, abase fear and thought.
sentences.s079-s1: Rarely in these times did it happen that a noble cypress came into bearing.
sentences.s079-s2: The delightful spring withered; his heart was given over to sorrow and suffering.
sentences.s079-s3: His belly grew fat and his body became heavy; his purple countenance turned saffron-colored.
sentences.s079-s4: To him the mother said, 'O my life (dear)! What is the matter with you that you have turned so pale?'
sentences.s079-s17: Zal uttered a long "afrin" of praise; he praised him abundantly and led him to prayer.
sentences.s079-s18: Thus he said to Zal, 'Why is this sorrow? Why are there tears within Hejbar's eye?'
sentences.s079-s19: From that silvery cypress with a moonlike face there comes a single male lion, Namjuy.
sentences.s079-s20: who would make Hejbar kiss the dust at his feet; even a cloud would not be able to pass over his head.
sentences.s079-s5: He answered thus: "Day and night I will keep opening my lips in a cry."
sentences.s079-s6: Truly, I have come to the summit; I cannot find a way/permission to bear this heavy burden.
sentences.s079-s7: You say I have turned to stone, my skin filled (hardened); and if someone is of iron, that quality is in him as well.
sentences.s079-s8: Thus, until the hour of childbirth came, she was in need of sleep and rest.
sentences.s079-s33: You will see many shoots and a crest upon its weary top; you will see its union that very day.
sentences.s079-s34: From that time on it possessed one of my feathers; the protective shade of my glory was auspicious.
sentences.s079-s35: This news should make you glad; you must present yourself before the sovereign.
sentences.s079-s36: For he gave you this royal tree, whose fate makes it bloom anew every day.
sentences.s079-s37: Do not let your heart be troubled by this matter, for your flourishing bough has come into fruit.
sentences.s079-s38: He spoke, and plucked one feather from his arm; then threw it and soared up on high.
sentences.s079-s39: Zāl rose and took up that feather; he departed and did what had been said — O marvel!
sentences.s079-s40: At that event the whole world watched: every eye full of blood and every soul weary.
